behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: The TSP approximation ratio is an algorithmic property independent of coordinate source. The failure (heuristic produces >1.5× optimal route) is purely computational. Testing the algorithm with diverse, systematically-chosen test coordinates (5, 10, 15, 20 stops; varying geometries) reveals if the implementation meets the approximation guarantee. No geocoding, no real infrastructure, no component wiring needed—the algorithm either works or doesn't.
behaviors.test_description: Create coordinate sets for the TSP solver with 5, 10, 15, and 20 stops in various geometries (clustered, distributed, linear). For each set, compute the heuristic-produced route length and establish an optimal or near-optimal baseline. Verify heuristic route length ≤ 1.5× optimal for all test cases.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: Time window calculation is pure mathematics: time = distance ÷ speed_for_road_type. The failure is a formula error or configuration lookup bug—both deterministic. No I/O, no dependencies, no orchestration required. Unit testing with known distance and speed pairs directly reveals any implementation flaw.
behaviors.test_description: Call the time estimator with multiple known distance/speed pairs from the road-type configuration (e.g., 100 km at 50 km/h → 2 hours; 50 km at 100 km/h → 0.5 hours). Verify each computed time matches the formula distance ÷ configured_speed.

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: Shift validation is pure logic: compare total_route_time > remaining_shift_hours, compute excess. The failures (wrong comparison operator, off-by-one in excess, missing rejection) are deterministic logical bugs. No I/O, no infrastructure, no component wiring needed.
behaviors.test_description: Call the validator with routes of varying total times: 1 hour below remaining shift (should accept), exactly equal to remaining shift (edge case), and 0.5–2 hours exceeding remaining shift (should reject with exact excess). Verify acceptance/rejection and excess amount correctness.

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is the service's error-handling orchestration: address list → geocoding calls (one fails) → skip decision → route optimization on remainder → skip report generation. This spans multiple modules. Unit testing a single skip-logic function would not reveal if the skip decision actually propagates through the service pipeline, or if the skip report is populated correctly. Integration testing with a mocked geocoding API (returning empty for one address) verifies the full service behavior without needing real network. The real failure is in the system logic, not infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the service with a mocked geocoding API configured to return empty results for one address in a 5-address list. Call the service and verify: (1) a valid route is produced for the 4 remaining addresses, (2) the skip report lists the correct address by name or index, (3) the skipped address does not appear in the final route. Test with the failing address at different positions (first, middle, last) and with 3–6 address lists.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: Duplicate detection and consolidation is a deterministic data-transformation problem: identify equal addresses, merge their item lists. The failure (visiting duplicate addresses, losing items, incorrect merge) is purely logical. No I/O, external services, or orchestration needed. Unit testing the consolidation logic with test data directly reveals if the implementation fails.
behaviors.test_description: Call the deduplication logic with an address list containing known duplicates (e.g., [addr1, addr2, addr1, addr3] where addr1 has 2 delivery items in its first occurrence and 3 items in its second). Verify the output has exactly 3 unique addresses, addr1 appears once with combined item count (5), and no address appears more than once. Test edge cases: all addresses identical, alternating duplicates, duplicates at list boundaries.
